summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that a Rollup plugin can use emitFile to write files outside the designated output directory by embedding ../ sequences after an arbitrary filename prefix, bypassing the isPathFragment validation.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should produce a proof file (evohunt-traversal-proof.txt) at the project root directory (one level above dist/), demonstrating that the emitFile API writes outside the configured output directory boundary.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh installed rollup@4.58.0, executed the build with the malicious plugin, and the proof file appeared at /tmp/evohunt-test/evohunt-traversal-proof.txt instead of inside /tmp/evohunt-test/dist/, confirming the path traversal.
verification_method: The verify.sh script checks for the presence of the proof file at the project root (outside dist/) and confirms it does NOT exist inside the dist/ directory. The script also verifies that the vulnerable isPathFragment and writeOutputFile code exists in the mounted target repository source.
evidence_produced_by_run: The proof file evohunt-traversal-proof.txt is written to /tmp/evohunt-test/ (project root) instead of /tmp/evohunt-test/dist/ (output directory).
The resolve() call in writeOutputFile resolves path.resolve('/tmp/evohunt-test/dist', 'subdir/../../evohunt-traversal-proof.txt') to '/tmp/evohunt-test/evohunt-traversal-proof.txt'.
The isPathFragment check in the target source at src/utils/relativeId.ts confirms it only performs prefix-based validation.
The writeOutputFile function in src/rollup/rollup.ts confirms no boundary check on the resolved path.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable tag, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
